LAN3107 Essential Workplace Communication
Effective Oral Interaction

Structure of Workplace Meetings — Revision Reference

💡 Tip: Click a numbered tab above to jump to that stage of the meeting.
📋 1. Opening the Meeting
1. Welcoming participants
  • Welcome to the meeting, everybody. Thank you for joining today. Shall we begin?
  • Welcome, everybody. Thanks for attending today's meeting. I think we can start now.
2. Stating the purpose of the meeting
  • The objective / goal / aim of our meeting today is to …
    Example
    The objective of our meeting today is to finalise the budget for next quarter.
  • The purpose of the meeting here today is to …
    Example
    The purpose of the meeting here today is to discuss our new eco-friendly store policy.
  • Today, we are going to discuss …
    Example
    Today, we are going to discuss ways to reduce our carbon footprint in the office.
3. Checking if participants have a copy of the agenda
  • Have you all got a copy of the agenda?
  • Does everyone have a copy of the agenda?
4. Starting the meeting
  • So, let's begin with our first agenda item …
    Example
    So, let's begin with our first agenda item, which is the new recycling programme.
  • Let's begin by talking about …
    Example
    Let's begin by talking about the feedback we received from our customers last month.
  • We are going to cover … agenda items.
    Example
    We are going to cover three agenda items: staffing, budget, and the new product launch.
5. Inviting an opinion
  • I would like to hear your opinions on this topic.
  • What's your position on this issue / idea?
  • What are your opinions on …?
6. Expressing an opinion
  • I strongly suggest / recommend …
  • Why don't we …?
  • Perhaps we should …
Example for opening a meeting
Good morning, everyone. Welcome to today's meeting. Let's start now. Does everyone have a copy of the agenda? [pause] The purpose of today's meeting is to discuss green measures to be implemented in the stores. We are going to cover two agenda items: ways to make our retail shops more eco-friendly and ways to promote our sustainable image to the community. Let's begin by talking about ways to make our retail shops more eco-friendly.
💡 2. Making Suggestions
Firm and Strong Tentative
  • I strongly recommend …
    Example
    I strongly recommend we switch to reusable packaging. It will save costs in the long run.
  • I suggest …
    Example
    I suggest organising a staff workshop on sustainability before the end of the month.
  • In my opinion, we should …
    Example
    In my opinion, we should invest in solar panels for our main store.
  • In my view, the only solution is …
    Example
    In my view, the only solution is to reduce plastic use across all departments.
  • Let's …
    Example
    Let's set a clear deadline so that everyone knows what to prepare.
  • How about …?
    Example
    How about conducting a workshop on risk management as our coming staff development activity?
  • It might be a good idea to …
    Example
    It might be a good idea to survey our customers first to understand their preferences.
  • May I suggest …?
    Example
    May I suggest we invite an environmental consultant to advise us on best practices?
  • Perhaps we should / could …
    Example
    Perhaps we should look at what our competitors are doing before making a final decision.
  • Shall we …?
    Example
    Shall we put this idea to a vote so we can move forward together?
  • What about …?
    Example
    What about introducing a rewards scheme for customers who bring their own bags?
  • What if …?
    Example
    What if we replace all plastic cups in the office with reusable ones?
  • Why don't we …?
    Example
    Why don't we run a trial for three months and then review the results?
  • Would it be suitable / possible to …?
    Example
    Would it be possible to get the data before our next meeting so we can be better prepared?
  • I am wondering if we could …
    Example
    I am wondering if we could partner with a local charity to promote our green image.
💬 3. Responding to Suggestions
Agreement Partial Agreement Disagreement
  • Yes, sure.
  • I think so.
  • That's what I'm going to say.
  • You're right.
  • I agree.
  • Absolutely.
  • That's a good idea.
  • I can't agree with you more.
  • That's an option, but …
    Example
    That's an option, but I think we should consider rescheduling instead of cancelling.
  • It's a possibility, but …
    Example
    It's a possibility, but we need to think about the extra costs involved.
  • Well, I see your point, but …
    Example
    Well, I see your point, but extending the deadline might delay the whole project.
  • I'm afraid I can't go along with you.
  • I don't think we could …
    Example
    I don't think we could finish the whole report by tomorrow. We need at least three days.
  • Sorry, I don't quite see it the same way.
  • I don't quite agree with you.
  • Sorry, that may not be possible.
  • I'm not sure about that.
Examples on making suggestions and responding to suggestions

Tracy: I suggest that we should reduce our paper usage. For example, we could replace paper bags with reusable or biodegradable alternatives. Do you think that's a good idea?

Chris: That's a great idea! We should also encourage customers to bring their own bags. Maybe we can offer a small discount for those who do …

Dave: Can we all agree that we should present these options to the management team?

Tracy: Sure. Let's do that.

Dave: Excellent! Let's move on to the second agenda item. What are your opinions on the ways to promote our sustainable image to the community? (Discussion continues)

🙋 4. Keeping the Discussion Going
Interrupting Politely interrupt a speaker when you have something to add
  • Excuse me, could I add something here?
  • May I jump in for a moment?
Asking for clarification If you do not fully understand a point, ask for clarification
  • Could you please clarify what you mean by …?
    Example
    Could you please clarify what you mean by "proactive"? Are you suggesting we start before the deadline?
  • Can you give an example of …?
    Example
    Can you give an example of what a flexible approach would look like in practice?
Asking for contributions Engage all members by asking for their thoughts
  • I'd like to hear your thoughts on this, [Name].
  • [Name], what do you think about this idea?
  • I would like to hear your opinions on this topic.
  • What's your position on this issue?
⚖️ 5. Discussing & Evaluating Options
Stating Preference / Advocating Your Ideas Evaluating Suggestions
  • I (would) prefer (not) …
    Example
    I would prefer not to make any changes until we have seen the full data.
  • I would rather (not) …
    Example
    I would rather not commit to a date until we have spoken to all the stakeholders.
  • My preference is …
    Example
    My preference is to roll out the new system gradually rather than all at once.
  • From my perspective, … is better.
    Example
    From my perspective, option A is better because it is more cost-effective in the long run.
  • However, …
    Example
    The new plan sounds exciting. However, we need to think carefully about the additional costs.
  • Although … / Whereas … / While …
    Example
    Although the idea is creative, I think we need more time to plan the details properly.
  • …, but …
    Example
    The proposal looks promising, but we need to consider the impact on our current budget.
  • On the one hand, … / On the other hand, …
🤝 6. Reaching a Decision
Recognising Common Ground Acknowledging the Need to Compromise
  • It seems that we can all agree that …
    Example
    It seems that we can all agree that reducing plastic use is the right thing to do.
  • It seems that we have a consensus that …
    Example
    It seems that we have a consensus that the event should be held in the second quarter.
  • We all concur that …
    Example
    We all concur that further research is needed before we commit to a final plan.
  • We are all in alignment that …
    Example
    We are all in alignment that customer satisfaction must remain our top priority.
  • It seems we have differing views, but maybe we could find a middle ground.
  • We don't quite agree on every point, so let's explore options that we could all support.
  • It seems like we have different options, so let's see if we can meet in the middle.
Compromising Revisiting the Agenda in the Next Meeting
  • On second thoughts, your suggestion may be more cost-effective / workable / practical. Let's make it our final decision then.
  • On further reflection, your idea seems more practical / sensible / feasible. I can go along with that.
  • It seems like we don't have time to make a decision today. Let's discuss the item again in our next meeting.
  • Let's revisit the agenda item again in the next meeting.
  • Rather than rushing to make a decision now, shall we revisit this item in our next meeting?
  • We can't reach a decision today, so let's carry this conversation over to our next meeting.
✅ 7. Closing the Meeting
Signalling the end of the meeting
  • It looks like we've run out of time, so I guess we'll finish here.
  • I think that's all we have time for today. Let's talk more about this next time.
  • I think we've covered everything on the agenda.
Checking for any other business
  • Is there any other business?
  • Does anyone have any other business they would like to discuss?
Summarising the discussion
  • To summarise, we've agreed to …
    Example
    To summarise, we've agreed to launch the pilot scheme in Store A starting next month.
  • Let me run through the main points we've agreed on.
  • So, to conclude, we've agreed that …
Thanking everyone for their participation
  • I think we've had a very productive meeting today and covered a lot of ground. Thank you so much for your input.
  • We've had a very fruitful meeting today and discussed a lot of issues. I would like to say thank you for your participation.
  • This has been an extremely effective meeting today, everybody. Thank you very much for all your suggestions.
Example for closing a meeting

A / B / C / D: Well, I think that's all we have time for today. We have some really promising ideas to take to management. Does anybody have anything else to contribute?

All: No. / I don't. / Not right now.

A / B / C / D: Well, if nobody has anything else to add, I think we can finish here. Thank you very much for attending the meeting and making contributions, everybody.

Now playing
